content:
9 min read
Just now

--

A measurement-driven look at whether a Rust-based, GPU-rendered editor is finally mature enough to replace VS Code as a daily driver in 2026.
It is 9 a.m., I open my work laptop, and VS Code is still showing “Activating extensions” while my coffee goes cold. VS Code runs on Electron (Chromium plus a Node.js runtime), which is why it can eat hundreds of megabytes of RAM before I type a single character. So I started asking a practical question: what would an editor feel like if it were built for 2026 hardware instead of 2015 web tech?
The problem with my current editor
Most of the slowness I feel in VS Code is not about features. It is about what the editor has to carry before I type a single character. That Electron foundation paid off for portability and extensions, but it also means I am running a browser engine to edit a file. The memory footprint and startup cost are there before any real work happens.
I notice it in three places. Startup is the obvious one, especially after an update or a fresh window. Extension activation is the next, when half a dozen language servers and linters wake up and the UI stutters while they load. The last one is editing large files, where scrolling and cursor movement start to lag in a way that breaks concentration.
None of this is about which editor looks cooler or has the better marketing. The real question is much smaller and much harder to ignore…
